title: Romans 13
subtitle: Submission to Authorities and the Day Is Near

sections.header: Verses 1–7 — "Let Everyone Be Subject to the Governing Authorities"

heb.paragraph: Exousia (vv. 1–3) denotes delegated authority or power. Paul declares that every governing authority has been ‘established’ (tetagmenai) by God — a perfect passive participle indicating a divine arrangement. The term covers all forms of civil government, not merely Roman rule. Paul’s argument is that the structure of governance itself is divinely ordained, even when specific rulers are unjust.

Diakonos in v. 4 describes the governing authority as God’s servant (diakonos theou) — an agent commissioned to carry out a specific function. The ruler is God’s servant for good and for the punishment of evil. The term carries no connotation of the ruler’s personal holiness but of their functional role in God’s ordering of society.

panels.ctx: Romans 13:1–7 is the most discussed NT passage on the relationship between Christians and the state. Written to believers living in the imperial capital under Nero (who had not yet begun persecution), Paul calls for submission to governing authorities as divinely established. The passage must be read alongside other NT texts that qualify it: Acts 5:29 (‘we must obey God rather than human beings’), Revelation 13 (the beast as a demonic parody of authority), and Paul’s own experience of unjust arrest and beating (Acts 16:37). Paul is not endorsing tyranny but recognising the general principle that ordered government is part of God’s providential care. The historical context may also reflect a concern that Jewish Christians returning to Rome after Claudius’s expulsion (AD 49) might resist Roman authority.

cross.note: Peter makes a parallel appeal: submit to every human authority for the Lord’s sake.
Peter and the apostles: ‘we must obey God rather than human beings’ — the qualification to unconditional submission.
God removes kings and sets up kings — the OT precedent for the principle of divine sovereignty over human government.

notes.note: Every existing authority is established by God. This is a theological statement about God’s sovereignty over human government, not a moral endorsement of every government’s actions.
The ruler is God’s servant to praise good and punish evil. When a government inverts this function — praising evil and punishing good — it has forfeited the legitimacy Paul describes.
Submission is motivated by both fear of judgment and conscience. The Christian submits not merely to avoid punishment but because it is right to honour God’s ordained structures.

Paul applies the principle concretely: pay taxes, revenue, respect, and honour. The mundane obligation of tax-paying is elevated to a theological act of recognising God’s providential ordering of society.

Calvin develops a theology of civil government that recognises its divine institution while allowing for resistance to tyranny through lower magistrates. The passage establishes the principle; wisdom applies it to complex circumstances.
Calvin notes that the ruler bears the sword not in vain — the state’s power of coercion is a legitimate function of divine governance, necessary in a fallen world.

The phrase ‘there is no authority except that which God has established’ (ou gar estin exousia ei mē hypo theou) uses a strong negation. The principle is universal but descriptive of the structure of authority, not prescriptive of any specific government’s policies.
The word machaira (‘sword’) refers to the short Roman sword and metonymically to the power of capital punishment or coercive enforcement.

Moo reads this passage as addressing a specific pastoral concern: Jewish Christians returning to Rome may have been tempted to resist Roman authority. Paul urges submission not as an absolute principle but as the normal Christian posture toward government in non-exceptional circumstances. The passage does not address situations of fundamental conflict between faith and state demands.

Schreiner emphasises that Paul’s command is grounded in creation theology: God established the institution of government as part of his providential ordering of the world. Submission to government is therefore a form of submission to God. However, when government commands what God forbids, Acts 5:29 applies.

sections.header: Verses 8–14 — "Put On the Armour of Light"

heb.paragraph: Plēroō in v. 10 (‘love is the fulfilment of the law’) echoes Jesus’ teaching that love fulfils the law and the prophets (Matt 22:37–40). Paul lists four commandments from the Decalogue and subsumes them all under ‘love your neighbour as yourself.’ Love does not replace the law but accomplishes its intention.

Kairos in v. 11 denotes the decisive eschatological moment. Paul urges ethical seriousness because ‘the hour has already come for you to wake up from your slumber.’ The nearness of salvation (v. 11) creates urgency: the night is nearly over and the day is about to dawn. This eschatological framing grounds ethics in the expectation of Christ’s return.

panels.ctx: Romans 13:8–14 transitions from civil duty to love as the fulfilment of the law, then to eschatological urgency as the motivation for holiness. The command to ‘put on the Lord Jesus Christ’ (v. 14) was famously the verse that triggered Augustine’s conversion (Confessions 8.12). The day/night imagery is common in early Christian paraenesis (cf. 1 Thess 5:1–11; Eph 5:8–14) and creates a sense of urgency: the eschatological dawn is imminent, so believers must live as children of the day.

cross.note: Jesus summarises the law in love for God and love for neighbour — the principle Paul applies.
Believers are children of the day, not of the night — the same day/night eschatological framework.
The entire law is fulfilled in one word: love your neighbour as yourself.

notes.note: The only continuing debt is to love one another. All other obligations can be discharged; the obligation of love is perpetual and inexhaustible.
Love is the fulfilment of the law. This does not abrogate the law but identifies love as the animating principle that energises proper law-keeping.
The eschatological motivation: the night is nearly over, the day is almost here. Ethical urgency is grounded in the imminence of Christ’s return.

The command to ‘put on the Lord Jesus Christ’ is the positive counterpart to ‘do not think about how to gratify the desires of the flesh.’ Holiness is not merely avoidance but active identification with Christ.

Calvin insists that love fulfils the law not by replacing it but by providing the disposition that produces genuine obedience. The law’s commands are not abolished but are obeyed from a heart of love.
Calvin notes that ‘putting on Christ’ means adopting his character, priorities, and way of life. It is the practical outworking of union with Christ.
The word eggyteron (‘nearer’) is a comparative form: salvation is closer now than when the Roman believers first believed. Paul lives with an expectation of the imminent return of Christ.
The phrase ‘make no provision for the flesh’ (pronoian mē poieisthe) means do not plan ahead to satisfy sinful desires. The command targets premeditated sin, not spontaneous temptation.

Moo reads this section as a twofold conclusion to Paul’s ethical instruction: love summarises the law’s demands (vv. 8–10), and eschatological urgency motivates their fulfilment (vv. 11–14). The ethical life is lived between the already of justification and the not yet of final salvation.

Schreiner argues that love as the fulfilment of the law demonstrates continuity between the Mosaic covenant and the new covenant. The law’s moral content remains authoritative; its demands are now met through Spirit-empowered love.
Schreiner emphasises the eschatological framework: ethical exhortation in Paul is never divorced from the expectation of Christ’s return. The nearness of the end makes moral urgency perpetual.

rows.label: vv.8–10
rows.text: Love as the Fulfilment of the Law

rows.label: vv.11–14
rows.text: Wake Up: The Day Is Near

lit.note: Romans 13 applies the living sacrifice to two spheres: civil obedience (vv. 1–7) and love-driven holiness (vv. 8–14). The eschatological urgency of vv. 11–14 undergirds both.

themes.note: Justice at 8 reflects the dual focus on civil authority and the law of love. Holiness at 8 captures the eschatological call to put on the armour of light. Sovereignty at 7 reflects God’s ordination of governing authorities.

ppl.name: Paul
ppl.role: Apostle to the Gentiles; author of thirteen NT letters
ppl.text: Paul (Saul of Tarsus) is introduced as a persecutor at Stephen’s stoning (Acts 7:58) and is dramatically converted on the Damascus road (Acts 9). He dominates Acts 13–28 across three missionary journeys, planting churches from Cyprus to Greece to Rome. His final journey to Rome as a prisoner frames Acts’ theological climax: the gospel reaching the empire’s capital, "boldly and without hindrance" (Acts 28:31).

ppl.name: Christ
ppl.text: Romans 13:1–7 is the most discussed NT passage on the relationship between Christians and the state…

trans.title: Comparing translations in Submission to Authorities and the Day Is Near

translations.text: Consequently, whoever rebels against the authority is rebelling against what God has instituted, and those who do so will bring judgment on themselves.
Therefore whoever resists the authorities resists what God has appointed, and those who resist will incur judgment.
Therefore, it is necessary to submit to the authorities, not only because of possible punishment but also as a matter of conscience.
Therefore one must be in subjection, not only to avoid God’s wrath but also for the sake of conscience.

debate: The phrase ‘there is no authority except that which God has established’ (ou gar estin exousia ei mē hypo theou) uses a strong negation. The principle…
Emphasises historical-critical, literary, or text-critical analysis.
Reads Submission to Authorities and the Day Is Near within the canonical framework of fulfilled prophecy and covenant theology.
Represented by Calvin, MacArthur, and the evangelical tradition.
Both readings engage the text seriously; they differ primarily in their hermeneutical starting points and assumptions about authorship and historical context.

thread.anchor: Submission to Authorities and the Day Is Near
thread.text: The entire law is fulfilled in one word: love your neighbour as yourself.

discourse.thesis: Submit to governing authorities as servants of God; love your neighbor as yourself, for love fulfills the law.

nodes.text: Let every person be subject to the governing authorities. There is no authority except from God, and those that exist have been instituted by God. To resist authority is to resist God's appointment; wrongdoers incur judgment. Rulers are not a terror to good conduct but to bad. Do what is good and you will receive approval. Authority bears the sword as God's servant to execute wrath on wrongdoers. Submit not only to avoid wrath but for conscience' sake.

children.text: Authority is God's servant (διάκονος) for your good — and an avenger (ἔκδικος) to bring wrath on evildoers.

nodes.text: This is why you pay taxes — authorities are ministers of God attending to this very thing. Pay to all what is owed: taxes, revenue, respect, honor.

Owe no one anything except to love one another, for the one who loves another has fulfilled the law. 'You shall not commit adultery, murder, steal, covet' — and any other commandment — are summed up in this: 'Love your neighbor as yourself.' Love does no wrong to a neighbor; therefore love is the fulfillment of the law.

children.text: Love does no wrong to a neighbor — therefore love is the πλήρωμα (fulfillment) of the law.

nodes.text: Do this knowing the time — it is the hour to wake from sleep, for salvation is nearer than when we first believed. The night is far gone; the day is at hand. Cast off works of darkness; put on the armor of light. Walk properly as in the daytime — not in carousing and drunkenness, not in debauchery and sensuality, not in quarreling and jealousy. Put on the Lord Jesus Christ and make no provision for the flesh.

discourse.note: Romans 13:1-7 is Paul's most extended treatment of the state. The context is the Roman church under Nero. Verses 11-14 were pivotal in Augustine's conversion ('put on the Lord Jesus Christ').
